prompt: You want to prevent a parent element's click handler from firing when a child button is clicked. Which method do you call?
options: event.preventDefault()
event.stopPropagation()
event.stopImmediatePropagation()
return false from the handler
explanation: `stopPropagation()` prevents the event from bubbling up to parent elements. `preventDefault()` prevents the browser's default action (e.g. following a link) but does not stop bubbling. `return false` in jQuery does both, but in native DOM only `stopPropagation()` stops the bubble.

prompt: What is the difference between a Docker image and a Docker container?
options: They are the same thing — different words for the same concept
An image is a read-only blueprint; a container is a running instance of an image
A container is stored on disk; an image runs in memory
Images are for Linux; containers are for Windows
correct_answer: An image is a read-only blueprint; a container is a running instance of an image
explanation: A Docker image is an immutable, layered filesystem snapshot — a template. A container is a live, isolated process created from that image with a writable layer on top. Multiple containers can run from the same image simultaneously, each with independent state.

prompt: Your users authenticate with JWTs. A user reports their account was compromised and you need to immediately invalidate their token. What is the problem with stateless JWTs?
options: JWTs cannot be decoded without the secret key
JWTs are stateless — the server has no record of them, so you cannot invalidate a token before it expires
JWTs automatically expire after 5 minutes
You can invalidate a JWT by updating the user's record in the database
correct_answer: JWTs are stateless — the server has no record of them, so you cannot invalidate a token before it expires
explanation: Stateless JWTs carry their validity period in the payload. The server verifies the signature without checking any database, so there is no server-side record to delete. Solutions: short expiry + refresh tokens, a token blocklist (denylist) in Redis, or rotating the signing secret (invalidates all tokens).

prompt: A data pipeline reads a large CSV file into a pandas DataFrame. What is the memory problem?
code_snippet:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv('logs.csv')
for chunk in df.iterrows():
    process(chunk)
options: `iterrows()` is not a valid method
The entire 10GB CSV is loaded into RAM before any processing begins
CSV files cannot be read with pandas
`iterrows()` is the most memory-efficient way to process large files
correct_answer: The entire 10GB CSV is loaded into RAM before any processing begins
explanation: `pd.read_csv()` without `chunksize` loads the complete file into memory. For large files, use `pd.read_csv('logs.csv', chunksize=10000)` which returns an iterator of DataFrames, processing one chunk at a time without loading everything at once.

prompt: What does a Conventional Commits message format like `feat(auth): add OAuth login` tell you?
options: Nothing — it is just a naming convention with no structure
The type (feat), scope (auth), and a short description — enables automated changelogs and semantic versioning
The file path that was changed
The branch the commit came from
correct_answer: The type (feat), scope (auth), and a short description — enables automated changelogs and semantic versioning
explanation: Conventional Commits define a structured message format: `<type>(<scope>): <description>`. Types like `feat`, `fix`, `chore`, `docs` allow tools to automatically generate changelogs and determine semantic version bumps (feat → minor, fix → patch, BREAKING CHANGE → major).

prompt: A developer uses an embedding model to power search. They upgrade to a new, better embedding model. What must they do with the existing vector database?
options: Nothing — embeddings from different models are compatible
Re-embed all documents with the new model, then replace the existing vectors in the database
Multiply the existing vectors by a conversion factor
Only re-embed documents added in the last 30 days
correct_answer: Re-embed all documents with the new model, then replace the existing vectors in the database
explanation: Each embedding model has its own vector space — vectors from different models are not comparable and live in incompatible spaces. Searching new query embeddings against old document embeddings produces meaningless results. When switching models you must re-embed everything and rebuild the index.

prompt: Which sorting algorithm is stable and has O(n log n) worst-case time complexity?
options: QuickSort
Heap Sort
Merge Sort
Selection Sort
explanation: Merge Sort is stable (preserves the relative order of equal elements) and guarantees O(n log n) in all cases. QuickSort is O(n²) worst case (though O(n log n) average). Heap Sort is O(n log n) but not stable. Selection Sort is O(n²).
